Machine Learning

Lora

3 min read
Just now

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1: TinyLlama LoRA adapter published on Hugging Face Hub.Large Language Models (LLMs) have become an important part of modern AI applications. While using pre-trained models is straightforward, understanding how to adapt them for specific tasks requires hands-on experience.
To strengthen my understanding of parameter-efficient fine-tuning, I recently completed a small learning project where I fine-tuned TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and published the trained adapter on Hugging Face Hub.
This project was focused on learning the complete workflow — from preparing the dataset to training, testing, and publishing the model.

Why TinyLlama?
I wanted to start with a lightweight model that could be trained on Google Colab without requiring expensive hardware.
TinyLlama is a good choice for learning because it supports instruction tuning while remaining relatively small compared to larger LLMs.
Instead of performing full model fine-tuning, I chose LoRA (PEFT), which trains only a small number of additional parameters while keeping the original model weights frozen. This makes fine-tuning much more memory efficient.

Preparing the Dataset
For this project, I created a custom Alpaca-style instruction dataset containing 123 instruction-response pairs.
The dataset covers different categories such as:

• General Question Answering

• Translation

• Summarization

• Basic Mathematics

• Python Programming

• Grammar Correction

• Text Classification

• General Knowledge
Each example follows the Alpaca instruction format:
### Instruction:
What is the capital of India?### Response:
The capital of India is New Delhi.This format makes the dataset suitable for instruction tuning.

Training Setup
The model was fine-tuned using:

• Base Model: TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0

• Fine-tuning Method: LoRA (PEFT)

• Framework: Hugging Face Transformers

• Trainer: Hugging Face Trainer API

• Hardware: Google Colab (Tesla T4 GPU)
Some of the training parameters included:

• Epochs: 3

• Batch Size: 2

• Gradient Accumulation: 2

• Learning Rate: 2e-4

• Maximum Sequence Length: 256
The training process completed successfully, and the LoRA adapter was saved for inference.

Challenges During the Project
Like most learning projects, this one was not completely straightforward.
I encountered multiple issues related to package compatibility, model loading, and environment configuration while working in Google Colab. Resolving these problems helped me better understand how the Hugging Face and PEFT ecosystem works.
These challenges were valuable because they provided practical experience beyond simply running a training script.

Testing the Fine-Tuned Model
After training, I tested the adapter using several prompts from the training dataset.
For example:
Prompt
### Instruction:
What is the capital of India?### Response:Model Output
The capital of India is New Delhi.The model was able to generate responses in the expected instruction format for the examples it had learned during fine-tuning.

Publishing on Hugging Face
Once testing was complete, I published the trained LoRA adapter on Hugging Face Hub.
Model Link:
https://huggingface.co/kkasim/kasim-tinyllama-lora-v1
Publishing the adapter makes it easier to reuse the fine-tuned weights with the original TinyLlama base model.

What I Learned
This project helped me gain practical experience with:

• LoRA fine-tuning using PEFT

• Preparing Alpaca-style datasets

• Tokenization and preprocessing

• Hugging Face Transformers

• Hugging Face Trainer API

• GPU-based training using Google Colab

• Saving and loading LoRA adapters

• Publishing models on Hugging Face Hub
More importantly, it gave me a better understanding of the end-to-end workflow involved in parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

What’s Next?
This is only the beginning of my journey in LLM fine-tuning.
My next learning goals include:

• Exploring QLoRA

• Training on larger instruction datasets

• Comparing different fine-tuning strategies

• Evaluating model performance using benchmark datasets
I’m looking forward to building more advanced Generative AI projects and continuing to learn through hands-on experimentation.
Thank you for reading!
